coredata.dc:title: Optical properties of geometrically optimized graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Bugajny, Paweł
Szulakowska, Ludmiła
Jaworowski, Błazej
Potasz, Paweł
coredata.dc:description: We derive effective tight-binding model for geometrically optimized graphene quantum dots and based on it we investigate corresponding changes in their optical properties in comparison to ideal structures. We consider hexagonal and triangular dots with zigzag and armchair edges. Using density functional theory methods we show that displacement of lattice sites leads to changes in atomic distances and in consequence modifies their energy spectrum. We derive appropriate model within tight-binding method with edge-modified hopping integrals. Using group theoretical analysis, we determine allowed optical transitions and investigate oscillatory strength between bulk–bulk, bulk–edge and edge–edge transitions. We compare optical joint density of states for ideal and geometry optimized structures. We also investigate an enhanced effect of sites displacement which can be designed in artificial graphene-like nanostructures. A shift of absorption peaks is found for small structures, vanishing with increasing system size.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Optical properties

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Geometry optimized, hexagonal (a) and (b), and triangular (c) and (d), GQDs with armchair and zigzag edges. Color of bonds indicate percentage changes in bond lengths comparing to a most typical interatomic carbon–carbon distance. Appropriate values of bond lengths indicated by numbers are listed in Table 1. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure caption, the reader is referred to the web version of this paper.)

Low energy spectra for different tight-binding models for armchair (a) and zigzag (c) hexagonal GQDs with N=222 and N=216 atoms, respectively. (b) and (d) are corresponding enlargements of states around Fermi energy. M 1 is the simplest tight-binding model with nearest neighbor constant hoppings, M 2 includes next-nearest neighbor hopping, M 3 is a model for geometrically optimized structures, M 4 is artificial graphene model with reduced edge hopping from model M 3,
t
2
= − 2.0
eV
, M 5 is artificial graphene model with enhanced edge hopping from model M 3,
t
2
= − 3.0
eV
. Red arrows indicate the strongest possible optically allowed transitions, labeled by numbers 1–4, between states with symmetries determined by irreducible representations of C 6v point group of hexagonal GQDs.

Low energy spectra for different tight-binding models for triangular zigzag GQD with N=222 atoms (left), and enlargement of the splitting of the degenerate shell (right). M 1 is the simplest tight-binding model with nearest neighbor constant hoppings, M 2 includes next-nearest neighbor hopping, M 3 is a model for geometrically optimized structures, M 4 is the artificial graphene model with reduced edge hopping from model M 3,
t
2
= − 2.0
eV
, M 5 is the artificial graphene model with enhanced edge hopping from model M 3,
t
2
= − 3.0
eV
. Red arrows indicate the strongest possible optically allowed transitions, labeled by numbers 1–3, between states with symmetries determined by irreducible representations of C 3v point group of triangular GQDs.

Electronic densities of initial (lower) and final (upper) states for optically allowed transitions 1–4, indicated in Fig. 2 for armchair hexagonal GQD. Transitions 2 and 3 are double degenerate, labeled as a and b.

Electronic densities of initial (lower) and final (upper) states for optically allowed transitions 1–3, indicated in Fig. 2 for zigzag hexagonal GQD. A transition 2 is double degenerate, labeled as a and b.

Electronic densities of initial (lower) and final (upper) states for optically allowed transitions 1–3, indicated in Fig. 3 for zigzag triangular GQD. Transitions 1 and 2 correspond to excitations from the valence band states to the degenerate shell, and, within identical energy within model M 1, from the degenerate shell to the conduction band states. All transitions 1–3 are double degenerate, labeled as a and b.
Optical joint density of states for (a) armchair and (b) zigzag hexagonal GQDs for all considered models. Transitions indicated by arrows in Fig. 2 are labeled by numbers.

Optical joint density of states for zigzag triangular GQD for all considered models (left), and enlargement of low energy transitions from the degenerate shell (right). Transitions indicated by arrows in Fig. 3 are labeled by numbers.
A shift of absorption peaks as a function of size for armchair (left) and zigzag (right) GQDs for model M 3 (a) and (b), M 4 (c) and (d), M 5 (e) and (f).
Bond lengths and integral hoppings used in model 3 for hexagonal and triangular GQDs from Fig. 1.
Optically allowed transitions between initial and final energy states belonging to irreducible representations of C 6v(left) and C 3v (right) point group.

head.ce:title: Optical properties of geometrically optimized graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Theoretical Physics, Wroclaw University of Technology, Wybrzeze Wyspianskiego 27, 50-370 Wroclaw, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Theoretical Physics, Wroclaw University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Advanced Research Complex, 25 Templeton Str., University of Ottawa, Ottawa, Ontario, Canada K1N 6N5

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Advanced Research Complex, 25 Templeton Str., University of Ottawa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We derive effective tight-binding model for geometrically optimized graphene quantum dots and based on it we investigate corresponding changes in their optical properties in comparison to ideal structures. We consider hexagonal and triangular dots with zigzag and armchair edges. Using density functional theory methods we show that displacement of lattice sites leads to changes in atomic distances and in consequence modifies their energy spectrum. We derive appropriate model within tight-binding method with edge-modified hopping integrals. Using group theoretical analysis, we determine allowed optical transitions and investigate oscillatory strength between bulk–bulk, bulk–edge and edge–edge transitions. We compare optical joint density of states for ideal and geometry optimized structures. We also investigate an enhanced effect of sites displacement which can be designed in artificial graphene-like nanostructures. A shift of absorption peaks is found for small structures, vanishing with increasing system size.

ce:section.ce:para:
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) reveal many peculiar electronic properties that are shape- and edge-dependent, which makes them promising candidates for building blocks of the future opto-electronic devices [1–3]. Within possible edge terminations of nanostructures carved from graphene honeycomb lattice along typical crystallographic directions, armchair and zigzag are most stable [4–7]. A prominent feature of zigzag edges is an appearance of strongly localized edge states, not present for armchair edge termination [8–15]. These edges states significantly modify energy spectra and in consequence affect their optical properties [8,16–21]. In particular, it was shown that the energy gap quickly closes with a size increase in hexagonal zigzag GQD, while in armchair GQDs follow a power law
E
gap
∼
N
− 1 / 2
, where N is a number of atoms [18]. This allows, by appropriate choice of GQD, for controlling of the energy of optical transitions in a wide range of photonic energies.

In real systems disorder is always present. Potential sources of disorder include vacancies, adsorbates, impurities, topological lattice defects (e.g. pentagons and heptagons), interaction with substrate, as well as imperfect edge fabrication. Some types of disorder exist in the bulk of the sample. Their effect can be visible e.g. in transport properties and density of states of graphene nanostructures [22–24], or in the energy level statistics [25] and quantum chaos-related properties [26] of graphene quantum dots. On the other hand, because of the presence of edge states on the zigzag edges, it is important to investigate also edge-related lattice distortions. Several variants of such modifications were investigated. For example edge roughness can be modeled by removal of random atoms from the edges. Influence of this type of disorder on the stability of edge states in large graphene quantum dots [27,28] and the transport properties of graphene nanoribbons [29] have been investigated. Also, the effect of random onsite potentials on the edge on the wavefunctions and density of states of graphene quantum dots [30] and graphene nanoribbons [23] were studied. Moreover, the edge reconstruction for graphene quantum dots with imperfect passivation was considered [31]. For nanoribbons, the influence of edge relaxation on their band structure was investigated [32].

In this work, we study how the electronic and optical properties of hexagonal and triangular graphene quantum dots are affected by the atom displacements in proximity of edges, caused by edge relaxation. Based on the structure relaxation carried out within density functional theory (DFT) methods, we derive an effective tight-binding model for geometrically optimized hexagonal and triangular GQDs with armchair and zigzag edges. Significant sites displacements occur in the vicinity of edges. We model them by appropriate changes of hopping integrals as in [24,32], and analyze influence of these changes on energy spectra and optical properties. An effect of enhanced displacement is also considered, which can be modeled in artificial graphene nanostructures [33].

In order to describe edge distortion occurring in real systems, we analyze the system's geometry relaxation using Gaussian software [34]. We have established that the effect of using different typical exchange-correlation functional in the calculation, including PBE, PW91 and hybrid B3LYP [35,36], on the lattice distortion is insubstantial. We have chosen to rely our further analysis on the PBE-based calculations, which exhibit an energy gap of the studied nanostructures in accordance to other works. Also, we exploit the system's symmetry in the choice of the wavefunction basis of our calculations. Relaxed armchair and zigzag structures are shown in Fig. 1 . Colored bonds between adjacent sites correspond to percentage changes of bond lengths in comparison to most typical interatomic carbon-carbon distance (corresponding to green color seen in the center of zigzag GQDs). For small hexagonal armchair GQD shown in Fig. 1(a), bond length changes are strong even close to the center of the dot and no green bonds are present. Up to
± 3 %
of bond length changes are seen in a proximity of edges of structures, with the strongest change visible close to the corners (see a color scale in Fig. 1). Appropriate values of bond lengths indicated by numbers in Fig. 1 are listed in Table 1 ..

Our goal is to derive appropriate tight-binding model describing these edge distortions. Tight-binding Hamiltonian is written as
(1)
H =
∑
i , j , σ
t
ij
c
i σ
†
c
j σ
,
where the operator
c
i σ
†
creates an electron on ith p
z orbital with spin σ, t
ij is a hopping integral which describes the probability of scattering of electron on jth p
z orbital ϕ
j to ith p
z orbital ϕ
i . We use Chebyshev polynomials expressions relating values of hopping integrals
t
ij σ
to the interatomic distances [37]. This allows us to introduce models with hoppings corresponding to appropriate colors in Fig. 1. Values of hoppings are listed in Table 1. We note here that a considerable increase of bond lengths has very weak effect on hopping change, while a small decrease of bond lengths can significantly change a hopping integral, see Chebyshev polynomials from Ref. [37]. Thus, while some colors of bonds slightly differ in a center of the dot, see darker and lighter green bonds in zigzag GQD in Fig. 1, we described them by one center-structure integral in order to maintain the simplicity of the model. These small differences do not affect results in a significant way. We also introduce some nomenclature: hoppings t 2 in armchair hexagon and zigzag triangle (orange bond) and zigzag hexagon (yellow bond perpendicular to the edge length) GQD indicated by number 2, we will call bulk–edge hopping, and refer to it when we consider later systems with artificially controlled site distances.

We analyze how extension of a simple tight-binding model with only nearest neighbors constant hoppings changes energy spectra of GQDs. We consider following models: (i) M 1 – only nearest neighbors with
t
1
= − 2.55
eV
, (ii) M 2 – nearest and next-nearest neighbors with
t
1
= − 2.55
eV
and
t ′ = − 0.1
eV
, (iii) M 3 – geometrically optimized hoppings for structures from Fig. 1 and hoppings from Table 1, (iv) M 4 – artificially reduced bulk–edge hopping from model M 3,
t
2
= − 2.0
eV
, (v) M 5 – artificially enhanced bulk–edge hopping from model M 3,
t
2
= − 3.0
eV
. Models M 4 and M 5 correspond to the so-called artificial graphene nanostructures, where appropriate deformations can be designed. In models M 3 to M 5 also constant next-nearest neighbors hoppings
t ′
are kept;
t ′
has a relatively small value comparing to nearest neighbor hopping and its changes due to geometry relaxation are negligible.

The low energy spectra for all models for hexagonal GQDs with
N = 222
(armchair) and
N = 216
(zigzag) atoms, respectively, are shown in Fig. 2 . We note that we shift in energy all spectra such that Fermi energy is always at
E = 0
. As the symmetry of the structures is preserved, the irreducible representations of C 6v point group of each energy level may be identified, see Fig. 2. We focus on few highest and lowest energy levels from valence and conduction bands, respectively. For armchair GQD, there are two single
A
1 , 2
(
B
1 , 2
)
and two double
E
1 , 2
degenerate energy levels in the valence similarly to zigzag hexagonal(conduction) bands. For zigzag GQD, there is one single
B
2
(
A
2
)
and two double
E
1 , 2
degenerate energy levels in the valence(conduction) bands. Models M 1 and M 2 give similar energy spectra. For geometry optimized structures within model M 3, several changes in comparison to models M 1 and M 2 are visible. For armchair GQD, while the highest (lowest) state from the valence (conduction) band does not significantly change in energy, the next energy level from valence (conduction) band shifts down (up) in energy. For zigzag GQD, two highest (lowest) states from the valence (conduction) band decrease (increase) slightly their energies. An effect is enhanced for model M 4 increasing relative distance between conduction and valence band states for zigzag GQD, while decreasing relative energy distance for armchair GQD. For model M 5 with larger bulk–edge hopping, an opposite effect to that in model M 4 occurs. We note that for armchair triangular GQD we observe similar effects to that for armchair hexagonal GQD and we do not study this structure further.

The low energy spectra for all models for zigzag triangular GQDs with
N = 222
atoms are shown in Fig. 3 . A characteristic feature of the spectrum is the presence of the degenerate shell in the middle of the energy gap [8–10,12–15], which is half-filled for charge neutral system. As the symmetry of the structures is preserved, the irreducible representations of C 3v point group of each energy level may be identified, see Fig. 3. We focus on two highest and two lowest energy levels from the valence and conduction bands with symmetry E, respectively, and the degenerate shell with four E and two single A 1 and A 2 states. The valence (conduction) band states increase (decrease) their energies for geometry optimized model M 3, while for artificially changed hoppings in models M 4 and M 5 the effect is much weaker. The degenerate shell splits when the second nearest neighbor hopping is introduced in model M 2, see right side of Fig. 3. There are no significant changes when geometry optimization effects are present within model M 3, and artificially decreased bulk–edge hopping in model M 4. The splitting of the degenerate shell is reduced within model M 5.

We investigate optical properties of GQDs by looking at the optical joint density of states calculated using dipole moments
< f | r | i >
connecting initial and final states with energies E
i and E
f
[18,38]. The strongest optically allowed transitions are shown by red arrows in Fig. 2 for hexagonal GQDs and in Fig. 3 for triangular GQDs, with summarized all possible transitions for corresponding point groups in Table 2 .

Electronic densities of states corresponding to each transition are plotted in Fig. 4 for armchair and in Fig. 5 for zigzag hexagonal GQD. For armchair hexagonal GQD, one can note stronger (weaker) overlap between electronic densities of initial and final states for transitions 1 and 4 (2 and 3). We note that the transitions 2 and 3 between weakly overlapping states are doubly degenerate...

For zigzag hexagonal GQD, edge- and bulk-like states can be distinguished, see Fig. 5. From three considered transitions, transition 1 has edge–edge character, transition 2 (double degenerate) has bulk–edge character, and transition 3 has bulk–bulk character. Edge–edge transitions seem to have the strongest overlap between electronic densities of initial and final states.

Electronic densities of states corresponding to each transition for zigzag triangular GQD are plotted in Fig. 6 . Transitions 1 and 2 correspond to excitations from the valence band states to the degenerate shell, and, with identical energy within model M 1, from the degenerate shell to the conduction band states. Within the degenerate shell there are only two optically active states, indicated by numbers 1 and 2 in Fig. 3 on the right. Transitions 1 and 3 have bulk–bulk character, while transition 2 bulk–edge character. All transitions 1–3 are double degenerate, labeled as a and b...

Fig. 7 shows low energy optical joint density of states (JDOS) for (a) armchair and (b) zigzag hexagonal GQDs. For both structures, the peak situated lowest in energy, related to transition 1, is the highest, marking transition 3 times (armchair) and 4 times (zigzag) stronger than the others. Transition 1 between two edge states (edge–edge) for zigzag is also stronger than between the two bulk states (bulk–bulk) for armchair GQD. This can be related to stronger localization of edge states and in consequence stronger overlap between initial and final states, comparing to bulk-like states, see Figs. 4 and 5. For armchair GQD, the similarly high peaks for transitions 2–4 show the weaker overlap of electronic densities of initial and final states. For zigzag GQD, however, peak 3 is higher than peak 2, which follows from the aforementioned overlap arguments..

Fig. 7 compares also optical properties of all 5 considered models. There are no visible differences between curves for models M 1 and M 2, as no significant changes of energy level positions have occurred (see Fig. 2). Geometry optimized model M 3 shows small shift of all peaks to higher energies. When the edge modification effect is amplified in model M 4, the main peak shifts to lower (higher) energies for armchair (zigzag) GQD. Opposite situation is observed for model M 5 with artificially enhanced bulk–edge hopping. An increase of bulk–edge hopping in model M 5 for armchair GQDs causes localization of bulk-like states in the center (not shown here), which increases its energy, and in consequence shifts an absorption peak to higher energies. On the other hand, in zigzag GQD, an increase of bulk–edge hopping in model M 5 enables strong localization, but it affects already localized edge states, therefore their energy does not change significantly. Oppositely, a decrease of hopping in model M 4 delocalizes the edge states, in this case resulting in the increase of their energy, which shifts up the absorption peak in energy.

Fig. 8 shows low energy optical joint density of states (JDOS) for zigzag triangular GQD. There are 3 peaks labeled by numbers 1–3 corresponding to transitions indicated by arrows in Fig. 3, and an extra, the strongest, lower energy transitions between states from the degenerate shell, which we enlarge in Fig. 8 on the right. We note here that we assume single occupation of states from the degenerate shell as in a predicted spin polarized case [9,10,12,13,15]. Transition 1 with states E from the degenerate shell has bulk–bulk character, see Fig. 6, and is much stronger than transitions 2 (bulk–edge) and 3 (bulk–bulk valence-conduction band transition). Due to overlap of absorption peaks within the degenerate shell, it is not possible to distinguish between them single transitions. While all the transitions were degenerate within model M 1, for models
M
2
−
M
5
they split making a peak much broader but also three times smaller, see Fig. 8 on the right. When the edge modification effect is amplified in model M 4, peak 1 shifts to higher energies, similar to zigzag hexagonal GQD. This is related to increase of energy of optically active states from the degenerate shell, see Fig. 3. Opposite situation is observed for peak 1 for model M 5 with artificially enhanced bulk–edge hopping..

The analysis of peak shifts with respect to a peak position from model M 2 as a function of hexagonal GQD size is presented in Fig. 9 for models M 3, M 4, and M 5. A common feature is that the effect vanishes with system size and for structures with around N=1000 atoms and
L = 3
nm
is negligible. As we consider edge effects, this can be related to a decrease of the number of edge to bulk atoms ratio as the size of the system grows. Additionally, the weakest shift is in general observed for the first peak. For models M 3 and M 5 peak shifts almost monotonically vanish with size, while for model M 4 a situation is more complex with an opposite shift direction for armchair hexagonal GQD, Fig. 9(c). We also note that we are not able to analyze peak shifts for triangular zigzag GQD due to increase of a number of possible optical transitions with size increase due to increase number of states in the degenerate shell..

We have theoretically investigated electronic and optical properties of geometrically optimized GQDs. Based on our DFT calculations, distortions of the atomic sites in the vicinity of the edges were observed and appropriate tight-binding model with modified hopping integrals describing these changes was derived. We have compared this model with simple tight-binding model with only nearest neighbors, and model with next-nearest neighbor hopping. We also considered enhanced effect of sites distortion by artificially increasing or decreasing corresponding bulk–edge hopping integrals. These last two models can regard to artificial graphene nanostructures. By considering structures with armchair and zigzag edge terminations, the role of edge states was analyzed. Basing on the geometrical changes preserving system's symmetry of hexagonal and triangular structures, corresponding irreducible representations of C 6v and C 3v point group of each energy level were determined and allowed optical transitions were identified. We have shown that sites distortions lead to changes in energy spectrum in a vicinity of Fermi energy and in consequence to a shift of absorption peaks in optical joint density of states. In zigzag GQD, we distinguished bulk–bulk, edge–edge, and bulk–edge like optical transitions, and relate their oscillatory strength to overlap between electronic densities of initial and final states. Due to strong localization of edge states, edge–edge optical transitions were shown to be the strongest. We have also investigated an edge distortion splitting of states from the degenerate shell in zigzag triangular GQD, showing that only two states having bulk-like character with the highest energy are optically active. We have shown that optical properties of GQD depend on system size and type of edge, and are weakly influenced by edge distortion with an effect vanishing with size increasing. On the other hand, we have shown that by designing artificially modified graphene nanostructures one can control a position of absorption peaks in a given system, shifting it to higher or lower energies. Finally, we note that our derived model M 3 can be used as a starting point to describe electron–electron interactions – as we show that atomic distances varies in a vicinity of edges it also changes relative Coulomb interactions between electrons occupying these sites.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Devrim Guclu for discussion. PP acknowledges partial funding from the Iuventus Plus program of the Polish Ministry of Science and Higher Education (Grant no. IP2012 007372). P.B. thanks to the partial financial support of the Polish National Science Center (MAESTRO 2014/14/A/ST3/00654).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: A.D. Güçlü, P. Potasz, M. Korkusinski, P. Hawrylak, Single-particle properties of graphene quantum dots, in: Graphene Quantum Dots, Springer, Berlin, Heidelberg, 2014, p. 39–90, http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/978-3-662-44611-9_4.
